text: The surgeon uses a four trocar technique for robotic colostomy reversal, with the patient in the supine lithotomy position and the robot docked over the left abdomen. A 5mm optical trocar is inserted through a right upper quadrant incision for intraperitoneal access, revealing minimal adhesions. A 12mm balloon trocar is placed in the right abdomen for the camera, while a 15mm trocar for the endo-wrist straight blur is positioned in the epigastrium, and an 8mm trocar in the right lower quadrant. The distal colon length is evaluated, and omental adhesions are lysed using a vessel sealer, followed by mobilization of the proximal colon to ensure adequate length.

The surgeon places the endo-wrist stapler into the intraperitoneal cavity, with visual cues indicating the grasping of the proposed plane of transection. The stapler blade is activated using the yellow pedal on the surgery console, completing the transection of the proximal colon. An epiploic appendage of the distal segment is secured to the sidewall to facilitate intercorporeal re-anastomosis. Silk sutures are used to approximate the proximal and distal colon segments, ensuring adequate laxity for tension-free re-anastomosis.

The surgeon creates a colotomy in the distal segment, revealing the colonic mucosa. A colotomy is then made in the proximal segment. The blades of the stapler are passed through both colotomy sites, with visual cues indicating readiness to fire. The common channel is closed using a running 3-0 PDS suture.

The surgeon reinforces the closure with interrupted 3-0 PDS sutures. Anesthesia injects 5 cc's of indocyanine green, revealing well-vascularized proximal and distal colon segments. The top of the suture line is over-sewn with a black silk suture.
